name: Questions (까 and 요)
summary: Ask questions in Korean using rising 요 intonation or the formal -습니까 ending, plus key question words.
explanation:
The easiest way to ask a question in Korean is to take a polite statement and raise your intonation. 밥 먹어요 means 'I eat'; 밥 먹어요? with a rising tone means 'Do you eat?'. No word order change is needed; the verb still comes last.

In formal speech, the question ending is -습니까/-ㅂ니까 instead of -습니다/-ㅂ니다. 갑니다 (I go) becomes 갑니까? (Do you go?), and 먹습니다 becomes 먹습니까?. This 까 ending is common in announcements, business, and polite service settings.

Question words sit naturally in the sentence rather than moving to the front like English. 뭐 (mwo, what), 어디 (eodi, where), 누구 (nugu, who), 언제 (eonje, when), 왜 (wae, why), and 어떻게 (eotteoke, how) drop into the normal slot: 어디 가요? (Where are you going?), 이게 뭐예요? (What is this?).
pattern: statement + 요? (rising tone)  |  formal stem + 습니까/ㅂ니까?
commonMistakes: Moving the question word to the front like English. 어디 가요? keeps the verb last; do not say 가요 어디.
Forgetting the rising intonation. In the polite 요 form, intonation alone signals a question.
Using -습니까 with the casual 요 ending. Pick one register: 가요? or 갑니까?, not 가요습니까.
Confusing 왜 (why) with 뭐 (what). 왜요? asks 'why?', 뭐예요? asks 'what is it?'.
Adding 까 to the polite 요 form. The 까 question ending belongs only to the formal 습니다 style.
tip: Easiest question hack: say the polite statement and lift your voice at the end. Save 습니까 for formal moments.
